lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Hence! home, you idle creatures get you home:

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Is this a holiday? what! know you not,

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Being mechanical, you ought not walk

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Upon a labouring day without the sign

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Of your profession? Speak, what trade art thou?

lines.speaker: FIRST COMMONER
lines.text: Why, sir, a carpenter.

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Where is thy leather apron and thy rule?

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: What dost thou with thy best apparel on?

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: You, sir, what trade are you?

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: Truly, sir, in respect of a fine workman, I am but, as

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: you would say, a cobbler.

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: But what trade art thou? answer me directly.

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: A trade, sir, that, I hope, I may use with a safe

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: conscience; which is, indeed, sir, a mender of bad soles.

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: What trade, thou knave? thou naughty knave, what trade?

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: Nay, I beseech you, sir, be not out with me: yet, if

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: you be out, sir, I can mend you.

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: What meanest thou by that? mend me, thou saucy fellow!

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: Why, sir, cobble you.

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Thou art a cobbler, art thou?

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: Truly, sir, all that I live by is with the awl: I

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: meddle with no tradesman’s matters, nor women’s matters, but with awl. I

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: am, indeed, sir, a surgeon to old shoes; when they are in

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: great danger, I recover them. As proper men as ever trod upon

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: neat’s leather have gone upon my handiwork.

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: But wherefore art not in thy shop to-day?

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Why dost thou lead these men about the streets?

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: Truly, sir, to wear out their shoes, to get myself into more

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: work. But, indeed, sir, we make holiday, to see Caesar and to

lines.speaker: SECOND COMMONER
lines.text: rejoice in his triumph.

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Wherefore rejoice? What conquest brings he home?

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: What tributaries follow him to Rome,

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: To grace in captive bonds his chariot-wheels?

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: You blocks, you stones, you worse than senseless things!

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: O you hard hearts, you cruel men of Rome,

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Knew you not Pompey? Many a time and oft

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Have you climb’d up to walls and battlements,

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: To towers and windows, yea, to chimney-tops,

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Your infants in your arms, and there have sat

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: The live-long day, with patient expectation,

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: To see great Pompey pass the streets of Rome:

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: And when you saw his chariot but appear,

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Have you not made an universal shout,

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: That Tiber trembled underneath her banks,

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: To hear the replication of your sounds

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Made in her concave shores?

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: And do you now put on your best attire?

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: And do you now cull out a holiday?

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: And do you now strew flowers in his way

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: That comes in triumph over Pompey’s blood?

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Be gone!

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Run to your houses, fall upon your knees,

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: Pray to the gods to intermit the plague

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: That needs must light on this ingratitude.

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Go, go, good countrymen, and, for this fault,

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Assemble all the poor men of your sort;

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Draw them to Tiber banks, and weep your tears

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Into the channel, till the lowest stream

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Do kiss the most exalted shores of all.

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: See whether their basest metal be not moved;

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: They vanish tongue-tied in their guiltiness.

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Go you down that way towards the Capitol;

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: This way will I: disrobe the images,

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: If you do find them deck’d with ceremonies.

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: May we do so?

lines.speaker: MARULLUS
lines.text: You know it is the feast of Lupercal.

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: It is no matter; let no images

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Be hung with Caesar’s trophies. I’ll about,

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: And drive away the vulgar from the streets:

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: So do you too, where you perceive them thick.

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: These growing feathers pluck’d from Caesar’s wing

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Will make him fly an ordinary pitch,

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: Who else would soar above the view of men

lines.speaker: FLAVIUS
lines.text: And keep us all in servile fearfulness.

stage_directions.text: Enter Flavius, Marullus, and certain Commoners.

glosses.definition: away; go home
manual labourers; craftsmen (not the modern 'mechanical')
what is your trade?
the carpenter's measuring stick, the badge of his profession
cobbler; quibble on 'souls' — he mends both shoes and (mockingly) people
rogue; rascal
don't be angry with me (with pun on out-at-heel shoes)
a shoemaker's pointed tool for making holes in leather; pun on 'all'
cow-hide; leather made from cattle
formal Roman procession honouring a victorious general; by Caesar's day a tightly-regulated political event
captive chieftains led in a Roman triumph to pay homage to the conqueror
prisoner's chains
Pompey the Great, Caesar's former ally then rival; defeated at Pharsalus (48 BCE) and murdered in Egypt; Caesar's current triumph is over Pompey's sons
anachronism — rooftops of ordinary English houses, not Roman
the river Tiber, which runs through Rome; personified as shaking with the crowd's noise
echo; reverberation
curved, hollow banks (that echo sound)
suspend; hold off
thanklessness — here, the plebs' disloyalty to Pompey's memory
silenced; unable to speak
the hill in Rome with the temple of Jupiter; Rome's political and religious centre
decorated with ceremonial ornaments — here, wreaths and garlands hung on Caesar's statues for his triumph
the Lupercalia, ancient Roman festival held February 15 — young priests ran naked through the streets striking women with goatskin thongs to promote fertility
the common people; the mob (Latin vulgus, no modern negative sense)

glosses.phrase: growing feathers pluck'd from Caesar's wing
glosses.definition: clipping Caesar's growing ambition, as one clips a hawk's pinion feathers to keep it from flying too high
a lower, more normal flight — a falconry metaphor
a slavish, cowed state of mind — the condition of subjects under a tyrant

annotations.title: Pompey, Pharsalus, and the shape of republican memory
annotations.body:
Marullus rebukes the crowd for celebrating Caesar while having cheered Pompey only a few years earlier. The charge lands only if the audience can hear what the tribune's invoking: the civil war of 49–45 BCE, which Caesar won by defeating Pompey at Pharsalus (48 BCE), after which Pompey was murdered in Egypt. Caesar's current triumph — Shakespeare's opening scene — is over Pompey's *sons*, the last holdouts of the senatorial cause.

The scene introduces the play's political physics without a history lecture. The Republic has already been injured. The conspirators will not spend the play defending an intact democracy against a tyrant; they will spend it trying to revive a Republic that Caesar has effectively already replaced. Marullus's nostalgia for Pompey is, in that sense, the play's first definition of its republican nostalgia in general — mourning something already gone.
sources: T. P. Wiseman, *Remembering the Roman People* (2009)

annotations.title: Growing feathers and servile fearfulness
annotations.body:
Flavius's closing image — 'These growing feathers pluck'd from Caesar's wing / Will make him fly an ordinary pitch' — introduces the play's recurring falconry and clipping metaphors. To the Renaissance audience this was technical language: a hawk's wing feathers could be trimmed to keep it flying low and under control. Flavius frames Caesar's ambition as a bird fledging — still clippable, but only for the moment.

The tribunes' fate, reported by Casca in 1.2 ('Marullus and Flavius, for pulling scarfs off Caesar's images, are put to silence'), is the first specific piece of evidence the play offers that Caesar has begun to punish political speech. What Brutus will frame as theoretical in his garden soliloquy — the worry that crowning Caesar would 'change his nature' — the tribunes have already touched the edge of.

annotations.title: Prose, plebs, and the stock of Roman crowd-scenes
annotations.body:
Opening in prose with a trade-joking cobbler sets a register Shakespeare will exploit twice more (the funeral crowds of 3.2, the lynching crowd of 3.3). The Roman 'plebs' in this play are not noble peasants; they are working-class punsters who bend their opinions to whatever rhetorician last addressed them. The cobbler's puns on 'soles/souls' and 'awl/all' are Elizabethan craftsmen-jokes, not Roman — yet another deliberate English inflection of the setting. Shakespeare makes the Roman street look and sound like Cheapside.

The contrast with Marullus's verse rebuke is the play's first lesson in rhetoric: elevated oratory pitched to an audience already in its holiday clothes, tired of tribunes and hungry for Caesar. It will be the same audience, eager for a show, that Antony plays in 3.2.
sources: Richard Wilson, *Julius Caesar: A Critical Casebook* (1992)

trials.prompt: Why are the tribunes Flavius and Marullus so angry with the commoners?
options: The commoners have refused to pay their taxes
The commoners are celebrating Caesar's triumph over Pompey's sons, though they once cheered Pompey himself
The commoners have committed a religious offence at the Lupercal
The commoners support a rival tribune

trials.prompt: The Second Commoner's 'mender of bad soles' does what in the scene's opening exchange?
options: States his trade plainly
Sets up a running pun on soles/souls and awl/all — the plebs' mode of indirect resistance to the tribunes
Announces his political allegiance
Mocks the audience

trials.prompt: What does Flavius's falconry image 'growing feathers pluck'd from Caesar's wing' imply about Caesar's political trajectory?
options: Caesar has already peaked and is in decline
Caesar is still a young bird whose ambition can, for now, be clipped — but soon won't be
Caesar has stolen feathers (metaphor for property) from the Roman people
Caesar intends to flee Rome

trials.prompt: Shakespeare's opening with the tribunes rather than with Caesar signals:
options: That Caesar is a minor character
That the play is concerned primarily with Rome's republican politics — not with Caesar the man, but with what his rise means for the state
That Shakespeare was uninterested in Caesar
That the actor playing Caesar wasn't ready

trials.prompt: What do the tribunes resolve to do about Caesar's statues?
options: Leave them alone, since Lupercal is sacred
Strip the ceremonial wreaths from them, even though it is the feast of Lupercal
Destroy them entirely
Add new decorations
